_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Mecklenburg County, NC. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses that shape what genealogists can and cannot find.
display_name: Mecklenburg County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Charlotte, NC — 832 E. Fourth Street (Mecklenburg County Courthouse; judicial and county office complex expanded repeatedly)

previous_courthouses.location: Charlotte, NC — intersection of Trade and Tryon streets (the colonial-era 'crossroads')
previous_courthouses.fate: Original colonial courthouse was a wooden structure, replaced multiple times. Early records survived transitions between buildings.
Mid-nineteenth-century courthouse survived the Civil War. Replaced as the city grew.
Late-nineteenth-century building served until replaced by the 1928 courthouse.

record_losses.cause: None — no major courthouse fire or war damage. Some early colonial records may be incomplete due to age and handling rather than destruction.
record_losses.description: Mecklenburg County was formed in 1762 and is one of the oldest counties in the western Piedmont. The courthouse has never suffered a major fire or war-related destruction. Records generally survive from formation, though some of the earliest colonial-era volumes show wear and occasional illegibility from age and handling. The county's age means its early records are critical for genealogy across a wide region — Mecklenburg was a parent or grandparent county for several surrounding counties.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Mecklenburg County's colonial-era records survive, which is remarkable for a county formed before the Revolution. If {ancestor_name} was in this area in the 1760s or 1770s, the deeds, court minutes, and tax lists from the county's earliest years may document {pronoun_possessive} family's presence.
record_losses.source: Tompkins, Daniel Augustus. History of Mecklenburg County and the City of Charlotte. 2 vols. Charlotte: Observer Printing, 1903.

deeds.completeness: Good — nearly continuous from county formation
deeds.gaps: Earliest deed books (1760s) show some wear and occasional difficult legibility but are substantially intact. No known volumes are missing.
deeds.repository: Mecklenburg County Register of Deeds, Charlotte; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records collection; Mecklenburg County online deed index

wills_estates.completeness: Good — wills and estate records from formation era
wills_estates.gaps: Some early loose estate papers are fragmentary. Bound will books are substantially intact.
wills_estates.repository: Mecklenburg County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives
wills_estates.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; NC State Archives digital collections

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage bonds from the colonial period
marriages.gaps: Bonds more complete than registers for the earliest decades. Some colonial-era bonds show age-related damage.
marriages.repository: Mecklenburg County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — County Court minutes from the Revolutionary era
court_minutes.gaps: Earliest volumes (pre-1780) have some gaps. Continuous from approximately 1780 forward.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Mecklenburg County Clerk of Court
court_minutes.online_access: NC State Archives microfilm; limited FamilySearch digitization

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1920. Charlotte city health department records may predate county registration.
vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives for delayed birth certificates

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1790–1940. Mecklenburg County is enumerated from the first federal census. Agricultural schedules, slave schedules (1850-1860), and manufacturing schedules add economic context. The county's large and growing population across two centuries makes the census a rich source.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families across time. Mecklenburg's status as a major population center means the census captures a wide cross-section of society in each enumeration.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Federal Census Schedules, Mecklenburg County, NC.

substitute_sources.description: Mecklenburg County was settled heavily by Scots-Irish Presbyterians in the 1750s-1770s. Early Presbyterian congregations — Hopewell, Providence, Sugaw Creek, Steel Creek, Poplar Tent — kept session minutes, baptismal registers, and membership rolls from the colonial period. These churches are among the oldest in the Piedmont, and their records document the founding generation of Mecklenburg County.
substitute_sources.repository: Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia; individual church archives; some microfilmed at Union Presbyterian Seminary (Richmond); NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Invaluable for Scots-Irish families. Presbyterian session minutes record baptisms (with parents' and sponsors' names), marriages, disciplinary proceedings, and membership rolls that provide family structure detail unavailable in civil records. The Mecklenburg Declaration of Independence signers (1775) were largely Presbyterian elders, and church records connect to that political history.
substitute_sources.source: Foote, William Henry. Sketches of North Carolina, Historical and Biographical. New York: Robert Carter, 1846.

substitute_sources.source_type: Charlotte city directories and urban records
substitute_sources.description: Charlotte published city directories from the 1870s onward, listing residents by name, address, and occupation. As Charlotte grew from a small town to the largest city in the Carolinas, these directories document the influx of families from surrounding counties and the development of distinct neighborhoods.
substitute_sources.repository: Robinson-Spangler Carolina Room, Charlotte Mecklenburg Library; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for identifying families in Charlotte between census years. City directories give annual snapshots of residence and occupation that fill the ten-year gap between federal censuses.
substitute_sources.source: Hanchett, Thomas W. Sorting Out the New South City: Race, Class, and Urban Development in Charlotte, 1875-1975. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1998.

substitute_sources.source_type: Mecklenburg Declaration of Independence records (1775 controversy)
substitute_sources.description: The disputed Mecklenburg Declaration of Independence (May 20, 1775) generated substantial documentary controversy. While the original document was reportedly destroyed in a fire, the reconstructed lists of signers and associated papers name leading Mecklenburg County families of the Revolutionary era. The controversy itself produced affidavits, witness statements, and correspondence that document colonial-era family connections.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; UNC-Chapel Hill Southern Historical Collection; published in numerous sources
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Useful for identifying prominent colonial-era families and their relationships. The signer lists and associated documents place specific individuals in Mecklenburg County in 1775 and connect them to family networks.
substitute_sources.source: Hoyt, William Henry. The Mecklenburg Declaration of Independence. New York: Putnam's, 1907.

narrative_hooks: Mecklenburg County was formed in 1762, and its records survive from the colonial period — if {ancestor_name} was in the Charlotte area before the Revolution, the deeds, court minutes, and tax lists from those earliest years may document a family that was here before there was a United States.

If {ancestor_name} was Scots-Irish Presbyterian in Mecklenburg County, the session minutes at Hopewell, Providence, or Sugaw Creek church may record {pronoun_possessive} baptism, marriage, or the discipline that a Presbyterian session imposed on members who strayed — a window into private life that civil records rarely open.

Charlotte's city directories from the 1870s onward list residents by name, address, and occupation — if {ancestor_name} moved to Charlotte, these directories track {pronoun_possessive} arrival and movement through the city year by year, filling the decade-long gaps between census records.

Before 1913, no North Carolina county required birth or death registration, so the absence of a birth record for {ancestor_name} in Mecklenburg County reflects the era — Presbyterian church records, census schedules, and city directories are the likeliest substitutes.

Mecklenburg County is old enough that its early records feed genealogy across multiple current counties — if {ancestor_name}'s family was in this area before the daughter counties were carved out, the trail starts in Charlotte.
source_references: Tompkins, Daniel Augustus. History of Mecklenburg County and the City of Charlotte. 2 vols. Charlotte: Observer Printing, 1903.
Hanchett, Thomas W. Sorting Out the New South City: Race, Class, and Urban Development in Charlotte, 1875-1975. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1998.
Foote, William Henry. Sketches of North Carolina, Historical and Biographical. New York: Robert Carter, 1846.
Hoyt, William Henry. The Mecklenburg Declaration of Independence. New York: Putnam's, 1907.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
